---
title: "Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)"
subtitle: "Minimum Wages and Employment: A Case Study of the Fast Food Industry in New Jersey and Pennsylvania"
author: "Corrine Chen"
date: today
execute:
  echo: false
---


## Introduction
Card and Krueger (1994) is one of the most influential and controversial papers in labor economics. The central question is simple but profound: **does raising the minimum wage reduce employment?**

Standard economic theory predicts yes, if you raise the price of labor above the market equilibrium, firms will demand less of it. But Card and Krueger challenged this prediction using a clever natural experiment.

In April 1992, New Jersey raised its minimum wage from \$4.25 to \$5.05 per hour, while neighboring Pennsylvania kept its minimum wage unchanged. The authors surveyed fast food restaurants in both states before (February 1992) and after (November 1992) the policy change, creating a natural **treatment group** (NJ) and **control group** (PA).

The key insight is the **Difference-in-Differences (DiD)** design:
$$
\text{DiD} = \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{NJ,\text{after}} - \bar{Y}_{NJ,\text{before}})}_{\text{NJ change}} - \underbrace{(\bar{Y}_{PA,\text{after}} - \bar{Y}_{PA,\text{before}})}_{\text{PA change}}
$$

The PA trend serves as the **counterfactual**, what would have happened to NJ employment had the minimum wage never changed. The identifying assumption is **parallel trends**: absent the policy, NJ and PA employment would have moved in parallel.

The finding was shocking to many economists: **employment in NJ fast food restaurants *increased* relative to PA after the minimum wage hike**, directly contradicting the standard prediction.

## Main Analysis

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

col_names = [
    'sheet','chain','co_owned','state',
    'southj','centralj','northj','pa1','pa2','shore',
    'ncalls','empft','emppt','nmgrs','wage_st',
    'inctime','firstinc','bonus','pctaff','meal',
    'open','hrsopen','psoda','pfry','pentree','nregs','nregs11',
    'type2','status2','date2','ncalls2','empft2','emppt2',
    'nmgrs2','wage_st2','inctime2','firstinc2','special2',
    'meals2','open2r','hrsopen2','psoda2','pfry2','pentree2',
    'nregs2','nregs112'
]

df = pd.read_csv('public.dat', sep=r'\s+', header=None,
                 names=col_names, na_values=['.'])

df['fte']  = df['empft']  + 0.5*df['emppt']  + df['nmgrs']
df['fte2'] = df['empft2'] + 0.5*df['emppt2'] + df['nmgrs2']
df['nj']   = (df['state'] == 1).astype(int)

df_v = df[df['status2'] == 1].copy()
df_v['delta_fte'] = df_v['fte2'] - df_v['fte']

nj = df_v[df_v['nj']==1]
pa = df_v[df_v['nj']==0]
```

### Table 2: Descriptive Statistics

Card and Krueger surveyed fast food restaurants in New Jersey (NJ) and Pennsylvania (PA) twice — before (February 1992) and after (November 1992) NJ raised its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05. Table 2 summarizes key variables by state.

```{python}
rows = []
for label, var in [
    ('FTE employment (wave 1)', 'fte'),
    ('FTE employment (wave 2)', 'fte2'),
    ('Starting wage (wave 1)',  'wage_st'),
    ('Starting wage (wave 2)',  'wage_st2'),
    ('Hours open per day (wave 1)', 'hrsopen'),
]:
    nj_val = nj[var].mean()
    pa_val = pa[var].mean()
    rows.append({'Variable': label,
                 'NJ': round(nj_val,2),
                 'PA': round(pa_val,2),
                 'Diff (NJ-PA)': round(nj_val-pa_val,2)})

pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Variable')
```

Before the policy (wave 1), NJ and PA stores looked very similar — comparable wages, employment levels, and hours. After the policy (wave 2), NJ starting wages jumped to \$5.08, confirming the minimum wage increase was binding. PA wages barely moved. Crucially, FTE employment in NJ *rose* slightly while PA employment fell — a first hint of the main finding.

### Table 3: Diff-in-Diff Estimate

The core of the paper is comparing the *change* in employment across the two states. If NJ and PA would have followed the same trend absent the policy, any divergence is attributable to the minimum wage increase.

```{python}
nj_b, pa_b = nj['fte'].mean(), pa['fte'].mean()
nj_a, pa_a = nj['fte2'].mean(), pa['fte2'].mean()
nj_ch = nj_a - nj_b
pa_ch = pa_a - pa_b
did   = nj_ch - pa_ch

se_did = np.sqrt(
    (nj['delta_fte'].std()**2 / len(nj.dropna(subset=['delta_fte']))) +
    (pa['delta_fte'].std()**2 / len(pa.dropna(subset=['delta_fte'])))
)
t_did = did / se_did
p_did = 2*(1 - stats.t.cdf(abs(t_did), df=len(df_v)-2))

table3 = pd.DataFrame({
    'NJ':    [round(nj_b,2),       round(nj_a,2),       round(nj_ch,2)],
    'PA':    [round(pa_b,2),       round(pa_a,2),       round(pa_ch,2)],
    'NJ-PA': [round(nj_b-pa_b,2), round(nj_a-pa_a,2), round(did,2)],
}, index=['1. FTE before (wave 1)', '2. FTE after (wave 2)', '3. Change (after - before)'])

table3.index.name = 'Variable'
table3
```

```{python}
print(f"SE of DiD: ({se_did:.2f})")
print(f"t-statistic: {t_did:.2f},  p-value: {p_did:.3f}")
```

The DiD estimate is **+2.72 FTE** (SE = 1.34, p = 0.043). PA employment fell by about 2.0 FTE over this period, reflecting general economic conditions. NJ employment bucked that trend and rose by 0.77 FTE. The difference — **2.72 full-time equivalent workers** — is attributed to the minimum wage policy, and is statistically significant at the 5% level.

### Table 4, Column (i): OLS Regression

The DiD can be expressed as a simple regression of the *change* in FTE on an NJ indicator:

$$\Delta \text{FTE}_i = \alpha + \beta \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{NJ}_i] + \varepsilon_i$$

The coefficient $\beta$ is the DiD estimate; $\alpha$ is the average change in PA.

```{python}
data = df_v.dropna(subset=['delta_fte']).copy()
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(data)), data['nj']])
y = data['delta_fte'].values

beta = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)[0]
resid = y - X @ beta
n, k = len(y), 2
se = np.sqrt(np.diag(np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X) * (resid**2).sum() / (n-k)))
t_vals = beta / se
p_vals = 2*(1 - stats.t.cdf(abs(t_vals), df=n-k))

reg_table = pd.DataFrame({
    'Coefficient': [round(beta[0],3), round(beta[1],3)],
    'Std. Error':  [round(se[0],3),   round(se[1],3)],
    't-stat':      [round(t_vals[0],2),round(t_vals[1],2)],
    'p-value':     [round(p_vals[0],3),round(p_vals[1],3)],
    'Sig.':        ['**','**'],
}, index=['Constant (= PA mean change)', 'NJ (DiD estimate)'])

reg_table
```

The NJ coefficient (+2.722) matches the Table 3 DiD exactly. The constant (−2.064) confirms that PA employment fell on average. Standard errors differ slightly from the paper because Card & Krueger used heteroskedasticity-robust SEs.

## Something Additional: Visualizing the DiD and Chain-Level Heterogeneity

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

ax = axes[0]
counterfactual = [nj_b, nj_b + pa_ch]
ax.plot([0,1], [nj_b,nj_a], 'b-o', lw=2.5, ms=8, label='New Jersey (NJ)')
ax.plot([0,1], [pa_b,pa_a], 'r-o', lw=2.5, ms=8, label='Pennsylvania (PA)')
ax.plot([0,1], counterfactual, 'b--', lw=1.5, alpha=0.5, label='NJ counterfactual')
ax.annotate('', xy=(1, nj_a), xytext=(1, counterfactual[1]),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='<->', color='green', lw=2.2))
ax.text(1.04, (nj_a+counterfactual[1])/2,
        f'DiD = {did:.2f}', color='green', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xticks([0,1])
ax.set_xticklabels(['Before\n(Feb 1992)', 'After\n(Nov 1992)'], fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel('Average FTE Employment', fontsize=11)
ax.set_title('Diff-in-Diff:\nNJ Minimum Wage Effect on Employment', fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

ax2 = axes[1]
chains = {1:'Burger King', 2:'KFC', 3:'Roy Rogers', 4:"Wendy's"}
results = []
for c, name in chains.items():
    sub = df_v[df_v['chain']==c]
    nj_c = sub[sub['nj']==1]
    pa_c = sub[sub['nj']==0]
    if len(pa_c) == 0: continue
    d = (nj_c['fte2'].mean()-nj_c['fte'].mean()) - (pa_c['fte2'].mean()-pa_c['fte'].mean())
    results.append((name, d))
names_c = [x[0] for x in results]
dids_c  = [x[1] for x in results]
colors = ['steelblue' if d>0 else 'tomato' for d in dids_c]
bars = ax2.barh(names_c, dids_c, color=colors, edgecolor='black')
ax2.axvline(0, color='black', lw=1)
ax2.axvline(did, color='green', lw=2, linestyle='--', label=f'Overall DiD = {did:.2f}')
for bar, val in zip(bars, dids_c):
    ax2.text(val+(0.3 if val>=0 else -0.3), bar.get_y()+bar.get_height()/2,
             f'{val:.2f}', va='center', ha='left' if val>=0 else 'right', fontsize=10)
ax2.set_xlabel('DiD Estimate (ΔFte_NJ − ΔFte_PA)', fontsize=11)
ax2.set_title('DiD Estimate by Fast Food Chain', fontsize=12)
ax2.legend(fontsize=10)
ax2.grid(axis='x', alpha=0.3)

plt.suptitle("Card & Krueger (1994): Minimum Wage & Fast Food Employment",
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The left panel shows the classic DiD visualization. PA employment fell over this period; the dashed line shows what NJ *would have done* absent the policy. Instead NJ rose, yielding a causal gap of **+2.72 FTE**.

The right panel breaks the result down by chain. Three of four chains (Burger King, Roy Rogers, Wendy's) show positive DiD estimates — the result is not driven by any single chain. KFC is the only exception. This robustness across chains strengthens the causal interpretation.

### Why Does DiD Enable Causal Inference?

DiD removes two sources of bias simultaneously. First, any **time-invariant differences** between NJ and PA (size, location, chain mix) cancel in the within-state first difference. Second, any **common time trends** — recessions, seasonal patterns, industry shocks — cancel in the cross-state second difference. What remains is attributable to the minimum wage policy alone, provided the **parallel trends assumption** holds.

Card and Krueger's result suggested that **monopsony power** in low-wage labor markets can explain why modest minimum wage increases need not reduce employment — a mechanism that has since gained broad empirical support and fundamentally changed how economists think about minimum wage policy.
